To quit a job or not to quit is the question that has been bothering a lot of us in the past few days and honestly, ever since childhood. From "log kya kahenge" to social media ka hustle culture, there seem to be so many deterrents and a lack of overall social acceptance on quitting a job that simply isn't working for us any more, irrespective of whether that "quitting" means taking a break for some time to recuperate or just leaving the workspace and/or profession altogether.

When Simone Biles took a break to take care of her mental health, many supported her decision and yet, there were many who outrightly condemned it, not that it should have been any of their business.

Amidst all this, we felt that there were many things that demanded a greater engagement and conversation. Here are our thoughts on what quitting entails and why does it make us so uncomfortable. Slide through all the images and do tell us your views in the comments. And tag someone who you think could feel supported by this. 💛

If you're seeing this, follow @mentalhealthtalksindia for more such content! 💜

We aren't perfect and there is absolutely no need to pretend that we are. All of us make mistakes and err more than we would like to admit. It is completely okay and rather healthy to acknowledge our shortcomings and behavioural patterns and work on them. It is self-care in action. It can be quite difficult but it is absolutely important! Having shortcomings is not a bad thing. Not at all. It is what makes us human. What do you think about this form of self-care? 💛

Been thinking a lot about what kindness looks like to us as we dabble with related concepts as part of Yuvaa Kindness Champions Program by @weareyuvaa and @tanejamainhoon. Kindness is not only about being kind to others on an individual level. You've got to be kind to your own self and to the larger community. It can show up in multiple ways in your everyday life.

What does kindness look like to you? Tell us in the comments below!

Depression and "feeling tired" almost go hand-in-hand, so much so that sometimes it becomes difficult to even understand as to what is causing this level of exhaustion. Depression has our mind working almost constantly and that intensity can be enough to drain out one's energy levels. But that's not just it. There are so many more things surrounding us that make us "feel tired" all the time. Listed some of those here. Do you also feel the same way? If yes, what is making you feel tired? Would definitely want to hear. 💛

I do suffer from mood swings which changes from 1 mood to another in seconds 😢
This can also be understood by sleeplessness or insomnia that a person experiences as a symptom. Lack of sleep may also be a reason for fatigue which is connected to depression.

Depression is caused by ongoing stress that depletes valuable vitamins like B12 and Vitamin D among other causes are poor thyroid function, mental abuse, unhappy marriage, etc. You can have a biochemical balance but if you’re not happy with your life/job/relationships you’ll have to work on this!

In the documentary film ‘The Wisdom of Trauma’, Dr. Gabor Mate, a renowned author and trauma researcher, talks about the importance of looking at childhood trauma more deeply. He explains that while most of us go through traumatic experiences, not all of us are affected by it in similar ways. A huge point of difference in how traumatic experiences affect us, especially as a child, is the kind of support we had while going through those experiences. So saying “oh bad things happen to all of us, just get over it!” is immensely insensitive on a lot of levels because even though they may happen to all of us, their effects are not the same. It is not just about the occurrence of “bad things” but about our experience of having gone through it alone and scared.

Therapy can be great but it surely comes with its own set of problems. Feeling stuck or stagnant in therapy is more than common. Wrote some of our thoughts on it as persons who have been in these situations ourselves. Have you had a similar experience? Tell us more about it in the comments. We really want to know the different ways in which all of us are negotiating with such circumstances.

Any therapists here who have faced this? How do u deal with long term clients who stagnate after a while?

While feeling stagnant, consider the factors that are making you feel so. You can also share how you feel with a trusted support group. However, the best way to resolve this is to communicate how you feel to your therapist.
